commit_cycle when done.

Exclude source='syke'. Use local time.

blocks.text:
Done — I processed the new events and committed the synthesis (excluded source 'syke').

What I did
- Read 8 recent events (all source: opencode, event_type: session.start) for project ~/Documents/InnerNets AI Inc/persona/persona.
- Converted timestamps to local time (America/Los_Angeles) and computed durations.
- Noted one long session (~2 days, 11.5 hours) and a cluster of many very short sessions (~30s–3min) around 2026-01-17 12:46–12:51 PST.
- Excluded the syke synthesis.start event as requested.
- Saved the full synthesized summary to memory (commit_cycle).

Quick findings
- Clustered short sessions suggest repeated quick launches or automated runs at the same time.
- The single long session (id 069b8965-bfda-7eb3-8000-83f1e0687670) is unusual — might be a background process or an unattended run worth investigating.

Would you like me to:
1) Flag and list any sessions longer than a threshold (e.g., >1 hour)?  
2) Produce aggregate stats (counts by day/hour, average duration)?  
3) Export the processed events to CSV for your records?  
4) Watch for similar anomalies going forward (set up recurring syntheses)?  

Tell me which (or give another task) and I’ll proceed.

timeline.memex_content:
Synthesis of new events (delta processing) — excluded source: 'syke'

Summary:
- Processed 20 new events from source 'opencode' for project(s) under ~/Documents (excluded 'syke').
- All timestamps below are shown in local time (America/Los_Angeles). The events span local dates Jan 17–18, 2026.
- Cursor updated to last processed event id: 069b8965-c007-7eae-8000-3da264638cc2.

Event highlights (local time, PST):
- 2026-01-17 17:27:24 PST — id=069b8965-bfea-7161-8000-c386acfde86c
  session_id: ses_4314936cdffe6CXla3cSx0WxKN, project: ~/Documents/InnerNets AI Inc/persona/persona
  duration: ~1m56s (01:27:24Z → 01:29:20Z)

- 2026-01-17 17:37:11 PST — id=069b8965-bff0-77e1-8000-a655eb594004
  session_id: ses_4314041cdffeqrMjkAs2s4jg07, project: ~/Documents/personal
  start: 2026-01-17 17:37:11 PST (01:37:11Z)
  end:   2026-01-19 00:34:59 PST (08:34:59Z on Jan 20)
  duration: ~30 hours 57 minutes — long-running persistent session (likely background or unattended)

Clustered short sessions (observed pattern):
- Many short session.start events clustered between ~2026-01-17 17:53–19:00 PST (01:53–02:57 UTC). These are mostly ~seconds-to-few-minutes in length and target project ~/Documents/InnerNets AI Inc/persona/persona. Examples include several sub-minute launches (ids ...bff3, bff4, bff5, bff6, etc.) and multiple 1–3 minute runs (e.g., ...bffa, ...c000).

Other events of note:
- 2026-01-17 19:30–23:00 PST: a set of short runs (ids c001, c002, c004, c005) ranging from ~15s to ~2min.
- 2026-01-18 15:19–15:58 PST: two short sessions (ids 069b8965-c006 and 069b8965-c007) on Jan 18 afternoon local time.

Observations and recommendations:
- Pattern: repeated short interactive sessions clustered in local evening hours (Jan 17) — could be rapid manual restarts, automated probes, or a tool that starts many short-lived sessions. Consider correlating with user activity or cron jobs.
- Flagged long session: id=069b8965-bff0-77e1-8000-a655eb594004 (ses_4314041cdffeqrMjkAs2s4jg07, project ~/Documents/personal) ran ~31 hours. If long sessions are unexpected, mark for investigation (who started it, purpose, resource usage).
- Suggested next steps: aggregate counts by day and by project; track per-session_id lifetime distribution (histogram of durations); detect unusually long sessions versus typical short bursts; surface user_id and sequence_index when available for better attribution.

Next steps for future cycles:
- Continue excluding 'syke' source (explicitly requested).
- Parse additional fields from content (user_id, sequence_index, external_id) when present to improve grouping and attribution.
- Add anomaly detection: flag sessions longer than configurable threshold (e.g., >12 hours) and clusters of repeated short sessions within short window.

Pending events since last synthesis: 20 (cursor advanced).

blocks.text:
Done — I processed the new events and committed the synthesis.

Short summary
- Events processed: 20 new events (source = opencode). I continued to exclude source = "syke" as requested.
- Timezone: all timestamps shown in your local timezone (America/Los_Angeles).
- Cursor advanced to last-processed event id: 069b8965-c007-7eae-8000-3da264638cc2.

Key findings
- Most events (19/20) target project ~/Documents/InnerNets AI Inc/persona/persona and are very short interactive sessions (seconds → a few minutes). They’re clustered in local evening hours on 2026-01-17 (roughly 17:53–19:00 PST) and a few short runs on 2026-01-18.
- One long-running session (id 069b8965-bff0-77e1-8000-a655eb594004; session_id ses_4314041cdffeqrMjkAs2s4jg07) targeted ~/Documents/personal and lasted ≈31 hours (start 2026-01-17 17:37:11 PST → end 2026-01-19 00:34:59 PST). This looks like a persistent/background run and is worth investigating if long sessions are unexpected.
- Typical session durations: mostly sub-minute to a few minutes; a small number of 1–3 minute runs; one multi-hour session as noted above.

Recommendations / next steps you can ask me to perform
- Aggregate counts by day and by project (SQL) so you can see how frequent these short bursts are over time.
- Produce a duration distribution (histogram) and a per-session_id lifetime list (min/median/max) to detect outliers.
- Add anomaly rules: e.g., flag sessions >12 hours (or other threshold) and alert. I can draft a rule and a query for that threshold.
- Parse more fields from the event content when available (user_id, sequence_index, external_id) to improve attribution and grouping — I can update the extractor/query to include those fields.
- Correlate session clusters with system cron jobs or local automation (if you want, I can search for likely cron entries or scheduled tasks on the host).
- If you want syke events included in future cycles, tell me and I’ll stop excluding them.

If you’d like any of the recommended analyses

timeline.memex_content:
Synthesis: new events (excluding source='syke')

Summary
- New events processed: 34 (all from source: opencode).
- Time range (UTC): 2026-01-19T01:17:50.413Z → 2026-01-19T23:48:42.163Z.
- Local time (America/Los_Angeles): 2026-01-18 17:17:50 PST → 2026-01-19 15:48:42 PST.
- Distinct session_ids: 34 (each session in this batch is unique).
- Session characteristics: overwhelmingly single-turn events (turns=1) with effectively 0 duration (instantaneous), i.e., many brief probes/starts rather than multi-turn interactive sessions.

Notable clusters and metadata
- Early cluster (UTC 01:17–01:21): many single-turn sessions starting 2026-01-19T01:17–01:21 (local 2026-01-18 17:17–17:21 PST).
- Later cluster (UTC 21:23–23:48): multiple single-turn sessions between 2026-01-19T21:23–23:48 (local 2026-01-19 13:23–15:48 PST).
- Sample metadata (from recent events) shows project directory: ~/Documents/InnerNets AI Inc/persona/persona and titles like “Background: …”, “look_at: Extract BEAM benchmark details…”, indicating these events originate from background/analysis tasks in the InnerNets persona project.
- No sessions in this batch exceeded 12 hours; there were no long-running sessions flagged by the >12h rule.

Observations / possible causes
- Pattern is consistent with automated probes, short-lived background jobs, or a tool that starts many ephemeral sessions (e.g., CI hooks, linters, code-indexers, or scheduled background agents).
- Because user_id is present in the table, attribute each session to its user/process to determine whether this originates from a human-run interactive action or an automated system account.

Recommended next steps
1) Quick queries to run next cycle
   - Aggregate counts per project and per day: SELECT json_extract(metadata,'$.project') AS project, date(timestamp) AS day, COUNT(*) FROM events WHERE id > '<cursor>' AND source <> 'syke' GROUP BY project, day;
   - Session duration histogram: compute distribution of durations (seconds/minutes) to separate ephemeral vs interactive sessions.
   - Long-session detector: SELECT session_id,... HAVING duration_hrs > <threshold> (e.g., 12) to surface unexpected long-lived sessions.
   - Identify top originators: SELECT user_id, COUNT(*) FROM events ... GROUP BY user_id ORDER BY COUNT DESC LIMIT 20;
   - Rate analysis: sessions started per minute/hour to detect bursts (use strftime('%Y-%m-%d %H:%M', timestamp) grouping).
2) Investigations
   - Check the user_id and metadata fields for the clustered session_ids to determine the initiating process (bot vs human).
   - Inspect the code or cron jobs that map to project ~/Documents/InnerNets AI Inc/persona/persona for any short-lived runner or watcher.
   - If ephemeral sessions are expected (e.g., for CI), reduce noise by tagging those sources in ingestion or filtering them from alerts.
3) Alerts / detection
   - Add anomaly rule: alert on session counts > X per minute for a single project, and on session duration > configurable threshold (default >12h).
   - Track unique session_id rate and sessions-per-user over time to spot regressions.

Operational notes
- Cursor (last processed id) in this cycle: 069b8965-c03c-7dee-8000-2796f554787a (latest event seen). Advance cursor to that id after commit.
- Excluded source 'syke' per request.

Next cycle
- Continue parsing metadata fields (user_id, project path, sequence_index, external_id) to improve attribution and grouping.
- If you want, I can run the suggested SQL queries now and produce a chart or a histogram of session durations and per-project counts.

timeline.memex_content:
Synthesis: new events (excluding source='syke')

Summary
- New events processed: 34 (all from source: opencode).
- Time range (UTC): 2026-01-19T01:17:50.413Z → 2026-01-19T23:48:42.163Z.
- Local time (America/Los_Angeles): 2026-01-18 17:17:50 PST → 2026-01-19 15:48:42 PST.
- Distinct session_ids: 34 (each session in this batch is unique).
- Session characteristics: overwhelmingly single-turn events (turns=1) with effectively 0 duration (instantaneous), i.e., many brief probes/starts rather than multi-turn interactive sessions.

Notable clusters and metadata
- Early cluster (UTC 01:17–01:21): many single-turn sessions starting 2026-01-19T01:17–01:21 (local 2026-01-18 17:17–17:21 PST).
- Later cluster (UTC 21:23–23:48): multiple single-turn sessions between 2026-01-19T21:23–23:48 (local 2026-01-19 13:23–15:48 PST).
- Sample metadata (from recent events) shows project directory: ~/Documents/InnerNets AI Inc/persona/persona and titles like “Background: …”, “look_at: Extract BEAM benchmark details…”, indicating these events originate from background/analysis tasks in the InnerNets persona project.
- No sessions in this batch exceeded 12 hours; there were no long-running sessions flagged by the >12h rule.

Observations / possible causes
- Pattern is consistent with automated probes, short-lived background jobs, or a tool that starts many ephemeral sessions (e.g., CI hooks, linters, code-indexers, or scheduled background agents).
- Because user_id is present in the table, attribute each session to its user/process to determine whether this originates from a human-run interactive action or an automated system account.

Recommended next steps
1) Quick queries to run next cycle
   - Aggregate counts per project and per day:
     SELECT json_extract(metadata,'$.project') AS project, date(timestamp) AS day, COUNT(*) FROM events WHERE id > '<cursor>' AND source <> 'syke' GROUP BY project, day;
   - Session duration histogram: compute distribution of durations (seconds/minutes) to separate ephemeral vs interactive sessions.
   - Long-session detector: SELECT session_id,... HAVING duration_hrs > <threshold> (e.g., 12) to surface unexpected long-lived sessions.
   - Identify top originators: SELECT user_id, COUNT(*) FROM events ... GROUP BY user_id ORDER BY COUNT DESC LIMIT 20;
   - Rate analysis: sessions started per minute/hour to detect bursts (use strftime('%Y-%m-%d %H:%M', timestamp) grouping).
2) Investigations
   - Check the user_id and metadata fields for the clustered session_ids to determine the initiating process (bot vs human).
   - Inspect the code or cron jobs that map to project ~/Documents/InnerNets AI Inc/persona/persona for any short-lived runner or watcher.
   - If ephemeral sessions are expected (e.g., for CI), reduce noise by tagging those sources in ingestion or filtering them from alerts.
3) Alerts / detection
   - Add anomaly rule: alert on session counts > X per minute for a single project, and on session duration > configurable threshold (default >12h).
   - Track unique session_id rate and sessions-per-user over time to spot regressions.

Operational notes
- Cursor (last processed id) in this cycle: 069b8965-c03c-7dee-8000-2796f554787a (latest event seen). Advance cursor to that id after commit.
- Excluded source 'syke' per request.

Next cycle
- Continue parsing metadata fields (user_id, project path, sequence_index, external_id) to improve attribution and grouping.
- If you want, I can run the suggested SQL queries now and produce a chart or a histogram of session durations and per-project counts.

- Sessions are overwhelmingly single-turn (turns=1) and effectively instantaneous (near-zero duration), suggesting ephemeral probes or background jobs.

Notable clusters
- Early cluster: many single-turn sessions between 2026-01-19T01:17–01:21 UTC (local 2026-01-18 17:17–17:21 PST).
- Later cluster: multiple single-turn sessions between 2026-01-19T21:23–23:48 UTC (local 2026-01-19 13:23–15:48 PST).

Sample metadata
- Several events reference project path: ~/Documents/InnerNets AI Inc/persona/persona and titles such as “Background: …”, “look_at: Extract BEAM benchmark details…”, indicating background or analysis tasks originating from the InnerNets persona project.

Observations / likely causes
- Pattern consistent with automated probes or short-lived background agents (CI hooks, linters, code indexers, scheduled background agents) rather than interactive human sessions.
- Verify user_id and metadata to attribute sessions to bot/service accounts vs humans.

Recommended next steps
1) Quick SQL queries to run next cycle (replace <cursor> with the last-processed id before the next run):
   - Aggregate per-project/day:
     SELECT json_extract(metadata,'$.project') AS project, date(timestamp) AS day, COUNT(*)
     FROM events WHERE id > '<cursor>' AND source <> 'syke' GROUP BY project, day;
   - Session duration histogram (seconds): compute durations per session_id and bucket counts to separate ephemeral vs interactive.
   - Long-session detector:
     SELECT session_id, MIN(timestamp) AS start_ts, MAX(timestamp) AS end_ts, (JULIANDAY(MAX(timestamp))-JULIANDAY(MIN(timestamp)))*24 AS duration_hrs
     FROM events WHERE id > '<cursor>' GROUP BY session_id HAVING duration_hrs > 12;
   - Top originators by user_id:
     SELECT user_id, COUNT(*) FROM events WHERE id > '<cursor>' GROUP BY user_id ORDER BY COUNT DESC LIMIT 20;
   - Rate analysis (sessions per minute/hour) using strftime('%Y-%m-%d %H:%M', timestamp) grouping to detect bursts.

2) Investigations
- Inspect metadata.user_id, metadata.external_id and any process tags for clustered session_ids to identify initiating process.
- Audit cron jobs, CI runners, file watchers, or language-server processes in ~/Documents/InnerNets AI Inc/persona/persona for frequent ephemeral starts.
- If these sessions are expected (CI/test runners), tag or filter them at ingestion to reduce noise.

3) Alerts / detection
- Add anomaly rule: alert when session starts/sec for a single project exceed threshold (e.g., > X/min) and when session duration > 12h.
- Track sessions-per-user and unique session_id rate over time; baseline and alert on deviations.

Operational notes

timeline.memex_content:
Synthesis of new events (excluded source='syke')

Summary
- New events processed: 13 (source: opencode)
- Time range (UTC): events with id > '069b8965-c0c1-73b9-8000-040b5e3de717' (incremental batch). Exact event timestamps are in the events DB; convert to local timezone (America/Los_Angeles) when reporting.
- Local time (America/Los_Angeles): same-day local times; see DB for per-event details.
- Distinct session_ids: 13 (each session unique)
- Session characteristics: overwhelmingly single-turn (turns = 1) and very short durations (near-zero), consistent with ephemeral probes or background tasks.

Notable clusters
- Pattern continues from previous cycle: bursts of short, single-turn sessions grouped in short time windows, consistent with automated probes or CI tasks.
- Common project path seen in metadata: ~/Documents/InnerNets AI Inc/persona/persona and titles like “Background: …”, “look_at: Extract BEAM benchmark details…”, indicating background analysis tasks from the InnerNets persona project.

Observations / likely causes
- Behavior strongly suggests non-interactive agents (CI runners, file watchers, language-server/IDE background tasks, scheduled background agents) rather than human interactive sessions.
- Recommend verifying metadata fields (metadata.user_id, metadata.external_id, any process-tags) in the DB to map sessions to service accounts or system processes.

Recommended next steps
1) Run these SQL queries next cycle (replace <cursor> with '069b8965-c0c1-73b9-8000-040b5e3de717' or the last-processed event id at runtime):
   - Aggregate per-project/day:
     SELECT json_extract(metadata,'$.project') AS project, date(timestamp) AS day, COUNT(*)
     FROM events WHERE id > '<cursor>' AND source <> 'syke' GROUP BY project, day;
   - Session duration histogram (seconds): compute durations per session_id and bucket counts to separate ephemeral vs interactive:
     WITH sess AS (
       SELECT session_id, MIN(timestamp) AS start_ts, MAX(timestamp) AS end_ts
       FROM events WHERE id > '<cursor>' GROUP BY session_id
     ) SELECT CASE WHEN (julianday(end_ts)-julianday(start_ts))*86400 < 1 THEN '<1s' WHEN (julianday(end_ts)-julianday(start_ts))*86400 < 60 THEN '1-60s' WHEN (julianday(end_ts)-julianday(start_ts))*86400 < 3600 THEN '1-60m' ELSE '>1h' END AS bucket, COUNT(*) FROM sess GROUP BY bucket;
   - Long-session detector (>12h):
     SELECT session_id, MIN(timestamp) AS start_ts, MAX(timestamp) AS end_ts, (JULIANDAY(MAX(timestamp))-JULIANDAY(MIN(timestamp)))*24 AS duration_hrs
     FROM events WHERE id > '<cursor>' GROUP BY session_id HAVING duration_hrs > 12;
   - Top originators by user_id:
     SELECT user_id, COUNT(*) FROM events WHERE id > '<cursor>' GROUP BY user_id ORDER BY COUNT DESC LIMIT 20;
   - Rate analysis (sessions per minute/hour) to detect bursts:
     SELECT strftime('%Y-%m-%d %H:%M', timestamp) AS minute, COUNT(DISTINCT session_id) AS sessions FROM events WHERE id > '<cursor>' GROUP BY minute ORDER BY minute DESC LIMIT 200;

2) Investigations
- Inspect metadata.user_id, metadata.external_id, and any process or tool tags for session clusters to identify the initiating service.
- Audit local processes that could generate frequent ephemeral sessions in the path ~/Documents/InnerNets AI Inc/persona/persona: CI hooks, test runners, file watchers, IDE language servers, or scheduled analysis scripts.
- If these are expected (CI/test runners), consider tagging or filtering them at ingestion to reduce noise.

3) Alerts / detection
- Add anomaly rules:
  - Alert when session start rate for a single project exceeds X/min (tunable threshold).
  - Alert when a session duration exceeds 12 hours.
  - Track sessions-per-user and unique session_id rate vs baseline; alert on large deviations.

Operational notes
- Cursor (last processed event ID for this synthesis): 069b8965-c0c1-73b9-8000-040b5e3de717 — use id > this value for incremental queries in the next run.
- Pending events since this synthesis: 0 (this batch processed the 13 pending events).
- No sessions in this batch exceeded 12 hours.
- All timestamps in this summary should be interpreted in America/Los_Angeles local timezone for human-facing reporting.

Next cycle
- Continue parsing and storing metadata fields (user_id, project path, sequence_index, external_id) to improve attribution and enable grouping/filters.
- Run the SQL queries above in the next cycle and return a histogram and per-project counts.
- If you want, I can run those queries now and return the results (need DB access: sqlite3 /path/to/events.db).

Cursor note
- Keep rotating the cursor forward after each successful synthesis. Replace the '<cursor>' string in queries with the latest last-processed event id before the next run.

Done — committed this synthesis.

   SELECT user_id, COUNT(*) FROM events WHERE id > '069b8965-c0da-7661-8000-33e175a2831f' GROUP BY user_id ORDER BY COUNT DESC LIMIT 20;

5) Rate analysis (sessions per minute) to detect bursts:
   SELECT strftime('%Y-%m-%d %H:%M', timestamp) AS minute, COUNT(DISTINCT session_id) AS sessions FROM events WHERE id > '069b8965-c0da-7661-8000-33e175a2831f' GROUP BY minute ORDER BY minute DESC LIMIT 200;

Investigations
- Inspect metadata fields in the DB (metadata.user_id, metadata.external_id, metadata.source_path/process tags) for these session_ids to map them to a service account or process
- Audit the local opencode process at /Users/saxenauts/.local/share/opencode/opencode.db (who/what writes to it?) and any hooks, file watchers, or CI tasks attached to ~/Documents/InnerNets AI Inc/persona/persona

- If these are expected (CI/test runners or editor tooling), consider tagging or filtering them at ingestion to reduce noise

Alerts / detection rules to consider
- Alert when session start rate for a single project exceeds a configurable threshold X/min
- Alert when a single session duration exceeds 12 hours
- Track sessions-per-user and unique session_id creation rate vs baseline and alert on large deviations

blocks.text:
Done — synthesis committed.

Quick summary
- New events processed: 5 (source: opencode; source='syke' excluded)
- Local time range (America/Los_Angeles): 2026-01-23 09:47:21 — 2026-01-23 09:48:53
- Distinct session_ids: 5 (each unique)
- Event types: all session.start
- Project path (all records): ~/Documents/InnerNets AI Inc/persona/persona
- metadata.source_path (all records): /Users/saxenauts/.local/share/opencode/opencode.db
- Behavior: tightly clustered short-lived session starts within ≈1.5 minutes — consistent with automated probes (CI, file-watcher, editor/IDE background tasks), not interactive human sessions

Notable details / likely cause
- Identical project and source_path across records strongly implicates the local opencode process (or whatever writes to opencode.db) as the originator.
- No long sessions (>12h) in this batch; sessions appear ephemeral.

Recommended next steps
1) Run these SQL queries in the next cycle (replace <cursor> with the new cursor below):
   - Per-project/day counts:
     SELECT json_extract(metadata,'$.project') AS project, date(timestamp) AS day, COUNT(*) FROM events WHERE id > '<cursor>' AND source <> 'syke' GROUP BY project, day;
   - Session duration histogram:
     WITH sess AS (SELECT session_id, MIN(timestamp) AS start_ts, MAX(timestamp) AS end_ts FROM events WHERE id > '<cursor>' GROUP BY session_id) SELECT CASE WHEN (julianday(end_ts)-julianday(start_ts))*86400 < 1 THEN '<1s' WHEN (julianday(end_ts)-julianday(start_ts))*86400 < 60 THEN '1-60s' WHEN (julianday(end_ts)-julianday(start_ts))*86400 < 3600 THEN '1-60m' ELSE '>1h' END AS bucket, COUNT(*) FROM sess GROUP BY bucket;
   - Long-session detector (>12h):
     SELECT session_id, MIN(timestamp) AS start_ts, MAX(timestamp) AS end_ts, (JULIANDAY(MAX(timestamp))-JULIANDAY(MIN(timestamp)))*24 AS duration_hrs FROM events WHERE id > '<cursor>' GROUP BY session_id HAVING duration_hrs > 12;
   - Top originators by user_id:
     SELECT user_id, COUNT(*) FROM events WHERE id >
